name: Cervest
funding: $34M+ (incl. $30M Series A, backed by Salesforce Ventures/Benioff)
summary: London-based Cervest built EarthScan, an AI platform that modeled physical climate risk (flood, heat, drought) for corporate assets. Despite being touted by the UK government as a flagship AI company, it entered administration in June 2023 after failing to pay most of its ~100 staff since April, having gone on a hiring spree just months earlier.
lesson: A hot narrative and marquee investors cannot substitute for burn discipline; leadership denial about an unsustainable cash trajectory (evidenced by a hiring spree right before collapse) turned a fixable problem into a sudden failure.
source: Proactive Investors, Trellis.net (2023 coverage of Cervest administration)

name: Descartes Labs
summary: A Los Alamos National Lab spinout, Descartes Labs built satellite-imagery and AI models for climate, agriculture, energy and defense analytics. After years of heavy cash burn without a durable path to profitability, majority control passed to private-equity firm Antarctica Capital, and in October 2024 it was folded into EarthDaily Analytics, losing its independent identity.
lesson: Deep-tech geospatial AI requires enormous, sustained capital for satellite and compute infrastructure; without a clear enterprise revenue engine, even technically impressive platforms end up absorbed into better-capitalized rivals at a fraction of their ambition.
source: Via Satellite, Geospatial World, PitchBook (2023-2024 Descartes Labs ownership coverage)

name: One Concern
summary: Stanford-born One Concern promised AI-driven, block-by-block disaster and climate-resilience predictions for cities, insurers and utilities. It raised large rounds and struck high-profile partnerships (including with SoftBank and SOMPO), but reporting and employee reviews describe a persistent gap between marketed prediction accuracy and delivered product value, alongside recurring layoffs.
lesson: Catastrophe-prediction AI is judged on real-world accuracy under real disasters; overselling model certainty to land marquee partnerships erodes trust faster than it builds a defensible business.
source: Glassdoor employee reviews, TechCrunch/Crunchbase funding coverage of One Concern

name: Nori
summary: Nori built a blockchain-and-data-driven marketplace for verifying and trading soil-carbon removal credits, aiming to be the transparent tracking layer for farmer-generated carbon. It shut down its exchange in 2024 as the voluntary carbon market's oversupply and credibility crisis collapsed demand for early-stage MRV marketplaces.
lesson: Building the tracking/verification infrastructure for a market ahead of durable buyer demand leaves a startup exposed when that underlying market corrects; infrastructure plays still need a market that shows up.
source: Carbon Pulse coverage of carbon-credit exchange shutdowns (2024)

name: Pachama
funding: ~$80M (incl. backers Serena Williams, Ellen DeGeneres, Amazon Climate Pledge Fund)
summary: Pachama used satellite imagery, LIDAR and machine learning to monitor and verify forest-carbon offset projects. Its own chief scientist quit over data showing the company continued selling credits from projects with rising deforestation, and after carbon-market prices collapsed, Pachama cut roughly 20 employees and was ultimately acquired by Carbon Direct in a distressed sale.
lesson: When your AI is the credibility layer for a market, internal doubts about your own model's integrity are an existential risk, not a PR problem — ignoring them undermines the product's entire reason for existing.
source: Investigative reporting on Pachama credit quality; Trellis coverage of Pachama layoffs; Carbon Direct acquisition announcement

name: Emitwise
summary: UK-based Emitwise built AI-driven carbon-accounting software to automate Scope 1-3 emissions tracking for supply chains. In mid-2025 it could not sustain independent operations and its technology platform was acquired by Green Project Technologies, which absorbed the product into its own carbon-accounting suite.
lesson: Carbon accounting became a crowded category almost overnight; without a durable differentiation or path to profitable scale, well-funded entrants still end up consolidated into larger competitors.
source: ESG Today, PRNewswire coverage of Green Project Technologies' acquisition of Emitwise (2025)

name: Indigo Ag
summary: Indigo Ag built AI/microbiome and remote-sensing tools including a large soil-carbon crediting program for farmers. After a down round and commercialization struggles, it cut roughly 80+ jobs in 2023, replaced its CEO, and restructured into smaller business units in 2024, with its ambitious carbon program scaled back well short of original projections.
lesson: Stacking an ambitious carbon-MRV business on top of an already capital-intensive core business multiplies execution risk; when growth capital tightens, the newer, unproven line is usually cut first.
source: AgFunderNews, Agriculture Dive coverage of Indigo Ag layoffs and restructuring (2023-2024)

name: Understory
summary: Madison, WI-based Understory built a proprietary ground-level hardware sensor network paired with AI models to deliver hyperlocal storm and hail data for insurers. The costly, self-owned hardware network model that defined its early years did not scale as planned, and the company pulled back to a much smaller, insurance-analytics-only footprint.
lesson: Owning and maintaining a physical sensor network is a capital-intensive, low-margin business layered underneath a software product; startups betting on proprietary hardware data moats need financing built for hardware timelines, not software ones.
source: Crunchbase, BusinessWire funding announcements; Understory company history

name: Xpansiv
summary: Xpansiv operated CBL, the largest voluntary carbon credit trading exchange, using data infrastructure to price and settle environmental commodities. As carbon-credit prices and trading volumes fell sharply from 2022-2023 amid credibility concerns across the voluntary market, the company saw declining liquidity and senior executive departures, undercutting its growth narrative and delaying a planned IPO.
lesson: Marketplace infrastructure businesses are only as strong as the underlying market's integrity; a credibility crisis in the commodity itself (carbon credits) drags down even the best-positioned exchange operator.
source: S&P Global, Carbon Pulse coverage of Xpansiv and voluntary carbon market volumes (2022-2023)

name: ClimateTrade
summary: Spain-based ClimateTrade built a blockchain-and-data marketplace for tracking and trading verified carbon offsets for corporate buyers. Like most carbon marketplaces, it was squeezed hard by the 2023-2024 collapse in voluntary carbon demand and credibility scrutiny of offset quality, sharply narrowing its growth trajectory.
lesson: Carbon-offset marketplaces built during a funding boom faced a structural demand air-pocket once buyers grew skeptical of credit quality — distribution alone can't fix a trust problem in the underlying asset.
source: Sifted, MIT Technology Review coverage of the 2023-2024 voluntary carbon market downturn

name: goodcarbon
summary: Berlin-based goodcarbon built a B2B data platform and marketplace to help companies source and track high-quality carbon credits. It scaled back sharply as the voluntary carbon market slump reduced corporate offset budgets and buyer appetite for early-stage marketplace intermediaries.
lesson: Early movers in a nascent marketplace category are exposed to broad category-level demand shocks that no amount of individual execution can fully offset.
source: Sifted Scout coverage of European carbon accounting and offsets startups (2024)

name: Patch
summary: Patch built infrastructure and an API-driven marketplace for carbon credit sourcing and retirement, aiming to be the tracking layer connecting buyers to verified removal projects. As the carbon-removal industry contracted amid the loss of federal support and weak growth capital, Patch's own CEO described the company being forced into a defensive pivot toward strategic partnerships rather than independent scale.
lesson: When policy tailwinds that underpinned a market's growth thesis reverse, infrastructure providers positioned for hypergrowth must be able to survive on much thinner volumes than their model assumed.
source: Trellis, Latitude Media coverage of carbon removal industry contraction (2025)

name: Spire Global
summary: Spire Global operates a satellite constellation delivering weather, maritime and aviation data with AI-driven forecasting products. Despite going public, the company disclosed substantial doubt about its ability to continue as a going concern in early 2025, restated prior financial statements, breached loan covenants, and had to sell its maritime business for cash just to shore up liquidity.
lesson: Satellite constellations are extraordinarily capital-intensive; going public doesn't fix a structurally negative cash-flow business, and financial-reporting failures compound investor distrust exactly when a company most needs market confidence.
source: Bloomberg Law, SEC filings, Motley Fool coverage of Spire Global's 2025 going-concern warning

name: South Pole
funding: Privately held; large multinational carbon project developer
summary: South Pole was the world's largest carbon-offset seller, using remote sensing and modeling to estimate and verify avoided-deforestation credits from projects like Kariba (Zimbabwe). Investigations found it had overstated the climate benefit of Kariba credits by a factor of five or more; the fallout cost it major clients (Volkswagen, Booking.com, Adyen), its CEO, and roughly 20% of its workforce in late 2023.
lesson: When a company's core product is a quantitative climate claim, the accuracy of that measurement is the entire business; papering over shortfalls in the underlying science destroys trust with an intensity proportional to how central that claim was to the brand.
source: Bloomberg, Follow the Money, MIT Technology Review coverage of the South Pole/Kariba scandal (2023)

name: Running Tide
summary: Running Tide combined ocean-based carbon sequestration (kelp, biomass sinking) with sensor buoys and AI-driven monitoring/verification to prove out and track carbon removal at sea, and was the first to sell ocean carbon credits. It folded in mid-2024 after failing to secure enough durable corporate buyers for its still-unproven MRV and removal approach.
lesson: Novel MRV science paired with a nascent buyer market is a double bet: even strong measurement technology cannot compensate for a market of buyers unwilling to pay for unproven removal claims at scale.
source: Canary Media coverage of climate tech company closures in 2024

name: Farmers Edge
summary: Canadian precision-agriculture company Farmers Edge combined satellite imagery, on-farm weather stations and AI analytics to give growers climate and yield risk insights. After a rocky SPAC debut, a Nasdaq delisting, and mounting losses, it filed for creditor protection under Canada's CCAA in 2023.
lesson: A SPAC listing provides a capital infusion, not a business model fix; public-market scrutiny of unit economics arrived faster than the company's ability to prove its weather-and-imagery-driven analytics could be delivered profitably at scale.
source: Reuters, Bloomberg coverage of Farmers Edge CCAA filing (2023)

name: PrecisionHawk
summary: PrecisionHawk built drone-collected imagery and AI analytics that fed weather- and climate-linked crop risk models for agriculture and infrastructure customers. After years of heavy spending on hardware and services infrastructure without matching revenue, it laid off most of its staff in 2022 and effectively wound down as an independent operating company.
lesson: Combining expensive hardware operations (drone fleets) with a data/AI analytics layer doubles the capital intensity of the business; when funding tightened, the company had no lean path to keep both halves alive.
source: Industry reporting on PrecisionHawk staff reductions (2022)

name: Rezatec
summary: UK-based Rezatec used satellite data and machine learning to assess climate and environmental risk for forestry, utilities and agriculture clients. It ceased trading amid funding difficulties after a decade of steady but insufficient commercial traction to justify continued investor support.
lesson: Long, capital-intensive incubation periods for geospatial AI need a credible path to a scaled enterprise sales motion well before investor patience for a 'promising but pre-scale' business runs out.
source: UK company filings and industry coverage of Rezatec's 2023 closure

name: Tomorrow.io
summary: Founded as ClimaCell and rebranded Tomorrow.io, the company built a proprietary weather-radar satellite constellation and AI forecasting platform, securing major enterprise, airline and government/defense (including US DoD) contracts, and acquiring rival forecaster Salient Predictions to expand its subseasonal capabilities.
lesson: Pairing proprietary data collection (owned radar/satellites) with a strong enterprise and government sales motion gives an AI weather company a defensible moat that pure software-only forecasters lack.
source: Tomorrow.io company announcements; TechCrunch coverage of DoD contracts and funding rounds

name: The Climate Corporation
summary: Originally WeatherBill, The Climate Corporation combined hyperlocal weather modeling with big-data agronomic analytics to price weather-based crop insurance and later a full farm-management platform. Monsanto acquired it for approximately $930M in 2013, one of the largest exits in agtech/climate-data history.
lesson: Turning a weather-data model into an insurance/pricing product that farmers directly pay for (rather than just selling data) created a defensible, monetizable business that strategic acquirers valued highly.
source: Widely reported 2013 Monsanto acquisition of The Climate Corporation (Reuters, TechCrunch)

name: Planet Labs
summary: Planet Labs operates the largest commercial Earth-imaging satellite fleet, providing daily imagery underpinning climate, agriculture, deforestation and disaster-risk analytics used by governments and enterprises worldwide, including acquired forest-carbon AI capability from Salo Sciences.
lesson: Owning the underlying data layer (daily global imagery) at scale lets a company continually layer new AI-driven climate products on top without rebuilding core infrastructure for each new use case.
source: Planet Labs public filings and investor communications

name: ICEYE
summary: Finnish company ICEYE built the world's largest constellation of synthetic-aperture radar (SAR) microsatellites, providing near-real-time flood, wildfire and natural catastrophe monitoring and AI-driven risk analytics for insurers and governments regardless of cloud cover.
lesson: Solving a genuine technical gap (all-weather, day-and-night radar imaging) that optical-satellite competitors cannot match created a durable differentiation in the climate-risk-monitoring market.
source: ICEYE funding announcements; industry coverage of SAR-based catastrophe monitoring

name: Jupiter Intelligence
summary: Jupiter Intelligence builds physical climate-risk models (flood, heat, wind, wildfire) used by insurers, government agencies and Fortune 500 companies to price and manage climate risk over multi-decade horizons, remaining an independent, growing player in the climate-risk-analytics category.
lesson: Focusing on long-horizon, scientifically rigorous physical risk modeling for regulated industries (insurance, real estate finance) builds a business with high switching costs and recurring enterprise revenue.
source: Jupiter Intelligence company announcements; DCVC investor profile

name: First Street Foundation
funding: Philanthropic and revenue-funded; commercial licensing model
summary: First Street built America's most widely used property-level climate risk model (flood, wildfire, heat, wind), integrating its Risk Factor scores into Zillow, Redfin and Realtor.com listings and generating substantial licensing revenue from financial institutions.
lesson: Distributing a climate-risk score through the platforms consumers already use (real estate listing sites) turned a research project into a de facto industry standard almost overnight.
source: First Street Foundation partnership announcements with Zillow, Redfin, Realtor.com

name: Cape Analytics
summary: Cape Analytics used aerial and satellite imagery with computer vision to assess property-level climate and catastrophe risk (roof condition, wildfire exposure, vegetation overhang) for insurance underwriting. Moody's agreed to acquire the company in January 2025 to strengthen its climate and catastrophe risk analytics offering.
lesson: Deep integration into insurers' underwriting workflows with narrowly-scoped, highly accurate risk attributes makes a climate-AI company an attractive acquisition target for large risk-analytics incumbents.
source: Moody's press release announcing the Cape Analytics acquisition (January 2025)

name: Sust Global
summary: Sust Global built a geospatial AI platform quantifying physical climate risk (wildfire, flood, heat, water stress) for financial institutions and corporates. ISS STOXX acquired the company in August 2025 to bolster its sustainable-investment and climate-risk data offerings.
lesson: Building climate-risk data that plugs directly into financial institutions' existing ESG and risk workflows creates a clean acquisition thesis for larger data and ratings providers.
source: ISS STOXX press release; ESG Today coverage of the Sust Global acquisition (2025)

name: Salo Sciences
summary: Salo Sciences combined satellite data, AI and ecological modeling to quantify forest carbon stocks and monitor deforestation risk through its Forest Observatory platform. Planet Labs acquired the company in January 2023 to build out its global forest-carbon dataset.
lesson: A specialized climate-AI capability (forest carbon quantification) built on a partner's data layer can become a clean, high-value acquisition once that platform wants to own the application layer directly.
source: Planet Labs press release on completing the Salo Sciences acquisition (January 2023)

name: Terrafuse AI
summary: Terrafuse AI built patented, highly granular wildfire risk prediction models using satellite data and machine learning. FutureProof Technologies acquired the company to combine its wildfire forecasting technology with FutureProof's AI-enabled insurance underwriting and pricing platform.
lesson: A narrowly-focused, best-in-class peril model (wildfire) is valuable enough on its own to be acquired by an insurtech platform seeking to bolt on differentiated risk data rather than build it in-house.
source: Pulse2 coverage of FutureProof Technologies' acquisition of Terrafuse AI

name: Kayrros
summary: French company Kayrros uses satellite imagery and AI to detect and quantify methane emissions and monitor energy/commodity infrastructure worldwide, becoming a leading independent source of emissions-tracking data for regulators, investors and energy companies.
lesson: Independent, satellite-verified emissions data has strong demand from regulators and financial markets precisely because it isn't self-reported by the emitters being measured.
source: Kayrros funding and valuation coverage in energy/climate trade press

name: GHGSat
summary: Canadian company GHGSat operates the highest-resolution commercial satellite constellation dedicated to measuring methane and CO2 emissions from individual industrial facilities, selling emissions-tracking data to energy companies, regulators and financial institutions.
lesson: Building first-mover, hard-to-replicate satellite sensing capability for a specific measurement problem (facility-level methane) created a durable data moat as emissions-disclosure regulation tightened globally.
source: GHGSat company funding announcements and industry coverage

name: Overstory
summary: Amsterdam-based Overstory uses high-resolution satellite data and AI to help utilities manage vegetation risk tree-by-tree, predicting and preventing wildfire ignitions and outages across 50+ utilities in North and South America and Europe.
lesson: Translating climate-risk AI into a specific, budgeted operational workflow (utility vegetation management crews) rather than a general risk score made the ROI concrete enough for utilities to pay at scale.
source: PRNewswire, ESG Today coverage of Overstory's $43M Series B (2025)

name: WindBorne Systems
funding: $26M+ (incl. $15M Series A led by Khosla Ventures)
summary: WindBorne builds long-duration, maneuverable smart weather balloons that fill critical atmospheric data gaps, feeding a proprietary AI forecasting model that has outperformed government forecasting agencies in independent comparisons, with customers including NOAA, NCAR and the US Air Force.
lesson: Cheap, novel hardware (reusable smart balloons) that closes a real data-sparsity gap can make an AI forecasting model measurably better than incumbents relying only on existing public data sources.
source: Axios Pro, TechCrunch coverage of WindBorne Systems funding and forecasting performance

name: Watershed
summary: Watershed built enterprise carbon-accounting software that automates emissions measurement across Scope 1-3 and helps large corporates (Airbnb, Spotify, Walmart) plan and report decarbonization, becoming one of the best-capitalized and most widely adopted platforms in the category.
lesson: Landing large, credible enterprise logos early in a new compliance-adjacent category creates a reference-customer flywheel that is difficult for later entrants to replicate.
source: Widely reported Watershed funding rounds and enterprise customer announcements

name: Windward
funding: Public company (listed on London Stock Exchange, 2024)
summary: Windward applies AI to satellite, weather and vessel-tracking data to deliver maritime risk intelligence, helping shipping, insurance and government customers anticipate weather-driven and geopolitical disruptions. The company completed a successful IPO on the LSE in 2024.
lesson: Combining weather data with other real-time signals (vessel movement, geopolitics) into a single risk product for a specific vertical (maritime) built a business durable enough to reach public markets.
source: Windward IPO coverage on the London Stock Exchange (2024)

name: Weather Analytics
summary: Weather Analytics built global historical and predictive weather datasets and models used for insurance, agriculture and energy risk pricing. DTN acquired the company in 2019 to expand its weather-data and analytics offerings for commercial customers.
lesson: Specialized historical weather-data assets are valuable acquisition targets for larger data platforms seeking to deepen coverage rather than rebuild data pipelines from scratch.
source: DTN 2019 acquisition announcement for Weather Analytics

name: Rebellion Photonics
summary: Rebellion Photonics built AI-powered hyperspectral gas-imaging cameras that continuously detect and quantify methane and other emissions in real time at industrial facilities. Honeywell acquired the company in 2019 to expand its industrial safety and emissions-monitoring portfolio.
lesson: Real-time, continuous emissions-tracking hardware paired with AI analytics solved both a safety and a carbon-accounting need simultaneously, making the technology attractive to a large industrial acquirer.
source: Honeywell 2019 acquisition announcement for Rebellion Photonics

name: Regrow Ag
summary: Regrow Ag (formed via the merger of Regrow and Dagan) uses satellite imagery, weather data and AI to measure and verify soil carbon and regenerative-agriculture outcomes at scale for food companies, ag retailers and carbon programs.
lesson: Focusing on rigorous, science-backed MRV (measurement, reporting, verification) for agricultural carbon rather than being a pure marketplace helped Regrow avoid the credibility collapse that hit several offset marketplaces.
source: Regrow Ag Series B funding announcements

name: Sylvera
summary: Sylvera uses satellite imagery, LIDAR and machine learning to independently rate the quality and durability of carbon credits, becoming one of the two leading carbon-credit ratings agencies alongside BeZero, serving corporates and asset managers evaluating offset purchases.
lesson: As the carbon market's credibility crisis deepened, independent third-party verification became more, not less, valuable — positioning as the trust layer for a troubled market can be a durable moat.
source: Sifted coverage of Sylvera's Series B funding round

name: Climatiq
summary: Climatiq built a developer-focused carbon calculation API and emissions-factor database that other software products embed to add carbon accounting features, positioning itself as infrastructure for the broader carbon-tracking software ecosystem rather than an end-user product.
lesson: Selling carbon-calculation infrastructure to other software companies (an API/B2B2B model) sidesteps direct competition with the crowded field of end-user carbon-accounting platforms.
source: Climatiq Series A funding coverage

name: Persefoni
funding: $100M+ (incl. Series B led by TPG Rise)
summary: Persefoni built an AI-assisted carbon management and disclosure platform designed for financial-grade accuracy, targeting the compliance needs created by mandatory climate-disclosure regulation (SEC, CSRD) for large enterprises and financial institutions.
lesson: Building for audit-grade financial rigor from day one, rather than a lightweight sustainability dashboard, positioned Persefoni to benefit directly as mandatory climate-disclosure regulation expanded.
source: Persefoni Series B funding announcements; TPG Rise investment coverage
